messages.content:
You are a technical writing assistant. You rephrase Python function docstrings while preserving their exact semantics.
Rules:
- Keep the function signature (name, parameters, return type annotation) exactly unchanged.
- Keep any import statements exactly unchanged.
- Keep doctest examples (lines starting with >>> or ...) exactly unchanged.
- Rephrase only the natural language description inside the docstring.
- Make each variant meaningfully different from the others.
- Return a JSON array of 4 strings, each containing one complete paraphrased function. No markdown fences, no other text.

def move_one_ball(arr):
    """We have an array 'arr' of N integers arr[1], arr[2], ..., arr[N].The
    numbers in the array will be randomly ordered. Your task is to determine if
    it is possible to get an array sorted in non-decreasing order by performing 
    the following operation on the given array:
        You are allowed to perform right shift operation any number of times.
    
    One right shift operation means shifting all elements of the array by one
    position in the right direction. The last element of the array will be moved to
    the starting position in the array i.e. 0th index. 

    If it is possible to obtain the sorted array by performing the above operation
    then return True else return False.
    If the given array is empty then return True.

    Note: The given list is guaranteed to have unique elements.

    For Example:
    
    move_one_ball([3, 4, 5, 1, 2])==>True
    Explanation: By performin 2 right shift operations, non-decreasing order can
                 be achieved for the given array.
    move_one_ball([3, 5, 4, 1, 2])==>False
    Explanation:It is not possible to get non-decreasing order for the given
                array by performing any number of right shift operations.
                
    """

samples.content:
["def move_one_ball(arr):
    """This function checks whether an array of N unique integers can be sorted into non-decreasing order by applying right shift operations any number of times. A right shift moves every element one position to the right, with the last element wrapping around to the first position. If such sorting is possible, return True; otherwise, return False. An empty array is considered sorted, so return True for that case.

For example:
move_one_ball([3, 4, 5, 1, 2])==>True
This is because two right shifts result in a sorted array.
move_one_ball([3, 5, 4, 1, 2])==>False
No sequence of right shifts can sort this array.
"""
    pass", "def move_one_ball(arr):
    """Given an array 'arr' containing N distinct integers in random order, determine if it can be transformed into a non-decreasing sorted array by performing right shift operations. You may perform right shifts any number of times. Each right shift operation shifts all elements to the right by one position, and the last element becomes the first. Return True if sorting is achievable, else False. For an empty input array, return True.

Example: move_one_ball([3, 4, 5, 1, 2]) returns True after two right shifts.
Example: move_one_ball([3, 5, 4, 1, 2]) cannot be sorted with any number of right shifts, so it returns False.
"""
    pass", "def move_one_ball(arr):
    """The task is to assess if an array of N unique integers can be sorted in non-decreasing order via right shift operations. Right shifts can be applied multiple times, where each operation moves elements one step to the right, cycling the last element to the front. If the array can be sorted this way, output True; if not, output False. The function should also return True for empty arrays.

For instance, with input [3, 4, 5, 1, 2], the answer is True because two right shifts achieve sorting.
For input [3, 5, 4, 1, 2], the answer is False as no right shifts can sort it.
"""
    pass", "def move_one_ball(arr):
    """Check if an array of N distinct integers, initially unordered, can be sorted into non-decreasing order by repeatedly applying right shift operations. A right shift operation rotates the array elements to the right by one position, with the last element moving to the beginning. Return True if such a sorted version exists, otherwise False. Note that an empty array is already sorted, so return True in that case.

Consider examples: [3, 4, 5, 1, 2] can be sorted to [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] with two right shifts, hence True.
[3, 5, 4, 1, 2] cannot be sorted by any right shifts, hence False.
"""
    pass"]
